responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

JAPA Prize Announcements

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association

JAPA Prize

The $1000 JAPA Prize is awarded to the author of the best paper published in the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association during the previous year. All publications, including review articles, research articles, essays and book essays, may be considered for the award. Candidates for the award are nominated by the members of the JAPA Editorial Board, with the final decision being made by the Editor-in-Chief.

JAPA New Author Prize

JAPA's $500 New Author Prize recognizes significant contributions to the journal by new authors. It is awarded for the best published manuscript by a new author, and is presented at the Winter Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

A new author is considered to be someone who has not previously published in JAPA or any other major psychoanalytic journal and whose psychoanalytic career is in its early stages. A mentoring/editorial group has been established to review submissions by new authors and assist in developing manuscripts for publication in JAPA.

Manuscripts must be submitted via the JAPA submission process, using the online system at http://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/japa. At the time of submission, authors should indicate in the cover letter that the paper is to be considered for the New Author Prize, and may ask that it be directed to the mentoring/editorial group for assistance in manuscript development. Final decision about publication remains with the Editorial Board and the Editor-in-Chief, as does selection of the annual prize-winning paper.

[[RunningHead]]

From the Editor: Comings and Goings

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

Email: mdwmd57@icloud.com

Mitchell Wilson  

JAPA benefits greatly from the careful and dedicated work of its Associate Editors, who write many of the decision letters to authors and shepherd papers through the review process. This past year Aisha Abassi, Anne Erreich, Jane Kite, Nathan Kravis, Katie Lewis, Stephen Portuges, and Donnel Stern have served ably in this role. Michael Garfinkle and Lynne Zeavin have been Acting Associate Editors on papers as well. Adelman and Jennifer Stuart are Co-editors of the JAPA Review of Books, and Rachel Boué Widawsky is Associate Book Review Editor for Foreign Books. We thank all of them for their efforts on behalf of JAPA.

We say goodbye to the following Editorial Board members, who have made significant contributions to the journal during the past three years and who rotated off the board on December 31: Phyllis Ackman, John Auerbach, Stanley Coen, Tiziano Colibazzi, John Muller, Jeffrey Prager, Avgi Saketopoulou, and Golan Shahar. Michael Levin and Gregory Rizzolo are rotating off as Editorial Associates. The following colleagues have been appointed to the JAPA Editorial Board for the term January 1, 2020, through December 31, 2022: Katie Aafjes-van Doorn, Rodrigo Barahona, Alison Brown, Michael Garfinkle, Karen Gilmore, Daniel Knauss, Chris Lovett, Elizabeth Lunbeck, Kevin Meehan, Gregory Rizzolo, Erika Schmidt, Joyce Slochower, Lynne Zeavin, and Kathryn Zerbe. Lara Sheehi and Matthew Steinfeld join us as Editorial Associates.

Mitchell Wilson

Email: mdwmd57@icloud.com

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Theory in Latin America

Práctica psicoanalítica: Un trabajo de resignificación y simbolización (Psychoanalytic Practice: A Task of Resignification and Symbolization). By Fanny Schkolnik. Montevideo: Rebeca Linke Editors, 2016, 267 pp., $18.00 paperback.

Michele Ain

Michele Ain

Juan María Pérez 2720, Apt. 402
Montevideo 11300
URUGUAY
Email: ainmichele@gmail.com

Michele Ain   Member, Uruguayan Psychoanalytical Association.

After qualifying as a psychiatrist, Fanny Schkolnik trained in psychoanalysis at the Uruguayan Psychoanalytic Association (Asociación Psicoanalítica del Uruguay), where she remains actively involved in the society's intellectual life. The author of numerous papers, several of which are republished here in Práctica Psicoanalítica, Schkolnik has long focused on understanding, through an intertwining of metapsychology and clinical experience, the forces that lead to psychic change.

In this book, neatly interwoven along three axes—narcissism and its boundaries, symbolization, and working through—the reader will find the central concerns of Schkolnik's psychoanalytic thinking discussed both candidly and in depth. In maintaining this open stance, she invites readers to question their implicit theories by conveying her own intellectual movement, creating a succession of “spiraling” progressive ideas around her reflections on clinical work.

Schkolnik studies symbolization in the psyche, a process that provides words for psychic traces that, despite their being foreign to the subject, affect the subject's life. Schkolnik reviews the concept of narcissism, considering its polysemic aspects and different forms of expression. The term narcissism has multiple meanings, which include a type of drive-directedness, an evolving state of libido, a libidinal answer to the loss of the object, and, finally, character pathology. In her clinical practice, Schkolnik takes all of these perspectives into account, adding her personal contributions to her understanding of the analytic process.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Emergency Remote Training in Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy: An Initial Assessment from Columbia

Justin Richardson , Deborah Cabaniss , Sabrina Cherry , Jane Halperin and Susan Vaughan

Susan Vaughan

21 West 12th Street, Suite A
New York, NY 10011
Email: jr195@cumc.columbia.edu

Susan Vaughan  

candidates, institutes, remote training, supervision, psychoanalytic education, distance learning, teleanalysis, training analysis, Covid-19

The Covid-19 pandemic and the social distancing required to combat it have set in motion an experiment in psychoanalytic education of unprecedented scope. Following an abrupt shift from in-person study to remote classes, supervision, clinical work, and training analyses, the Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research polled its psychotherapy and psychoanalysis trainees to assess their initial experience of remote training. Most candidates found the technical aspects of online learning easy and were satisfied with remote training overall. Across all programs, most trainees considered class length and reading load about right and felt their class participation was unaffected, though they found it harder to concentrate. Most found it no harder to start a training case, felt the shift to remote supervision had no negative effect, and were satisfied with seeing their training analyst remotely. Most trainees preferred in-person classes, clinical work, and training analyses to those offered remotely, yet in light of the health risks they said they were less likely to continue training in fall 2020 if in-person work resumed. Trainees suggested several modifications of teaching techniques to improve their participation and concentration in class. These findings' implications for the debate regarding remote training in psychoanalysis are explored.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Film

Critical Flicker Fusion: Psychoanalysis at the Movies. By William Fried. London: Karnac Books, 2017, xxiv + 160 pp., $34.95 paperback.

Richard Waugaman

Richard Waugaman

4701 Willard Avenue, #204
Chevy Chase, MD 20815
Email: richard.waugaman@georgetown.edu

Richard Waugaman   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Georgetown University; Training and Supervising Analyst, Emeritus, Washington Baltimore Psychoanalytic Institute.

William Fried's book offers a valuable psychoanalytic perspective on thirteen films, grouped in the categories of Secrets; Time and Death; Love and Lust; and Human Identity. He underlines his analytic approach by treating films as though they were sessions, and making connections between films and dreams (“the methods to interpret any tend to be relevant to all” [p. xxiii]). He admits his bias is to write about films he likes, and “to conceive the task of criticism as the enhancement of understanding, appreciation, and pleasure” (p. 119), rather than fault-finding. He explains that he originally wrote his essays, over a span of twenty years, as presentations at conferences where the films in question were first shown to the audience, so the subsequent discussion benefited from that collectively shared experience. He adds three brief but wide-ranging appendices, written for this book.

Fried may be influenced by his previous profession as a teacher of literature when he cautions against treating the film's characters as though they are real people with an unconscious; or treating the film's narrative as saying something about its screenwriter or director. I suspect he suffers from the faddish but flawed (and anti-psychoanalytic) literary theory that would promote this skewed “death of the author” view as dogma we all must follow (fortunately, Freud did not, in his brilliant works in applied analysis). Researching films usually uncovers meaningful links with the lives and conflicts of the screenwriter or director, or of the writer whose novel inspired the film. One of the most powerful applications of our psychoanalytic understanding is in elucidating such links. Fried ignores this dimension of the films he discusses (except with regard to the biographical film Gods and Monsters).

By contrast, Fried's exploration of films is greatly enriched by the close reading approach to literature, which parallels close listening in psychoanalysis.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud in Social and Historical Context

Freud: The Unconscious and World Affairs. By René Major and Chantal Talagrand; translated by Agnes Jacobs. New York: Routledge, 2018, xvi + 220 pp., $37.95 paperback.

Arnold Richards

Arnold Richards

200 East 89th Street, #45b/c
New York, NY 10128
Email: arniedr@aol.com

Arnold Richards   Former Editor, JAPA; faculty, Tongji Medical College, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, China; publisher, internationalpsychoanalysis.net and IPBooks.

Freud: The Unconscious and World Affairs, by René Major and Chantal Talagrand, is a new French biography of Freud that was recently translated into English. René Major practices psychoanalysis in Paris and has published a number of books. He was director of the Institut de psychanalyse de Paris and founded the journal Confrontation, which sought to link art, literature, philosophy, and psychoanalysis, an effort that also informs his book about Freud. Chantal Talagrand is also a psychoanalyst practicing in Paris. She teaches at the Institut des hautes etudes en psychoanalyse, “where she conducts a seminar on the relation between psychoanalysis, criminology, and law” (p. xi). Former chief editor of the magazines Cahiers, Confrontation, and Contretemps, she has also published many articles.

A considerable number of major biographies of Freud have appeared since the first, by Fritz Wittels, which has largely been forgotten, and the hagiographic three volumes written by Ernest Jones (1953-1957), which I think give a better account of its subject's life than of his theories. The book under review follows two other recent biographies: Elizabeth Roudinesco's Freud: In His Time and Ours (2016) and Joel Whitebook's Freud: An Intellectual Biography (2017). This resurgent interest in Freud's life calls for some explanation, but a persuasive one is hard to come by. Why go back to Freud? I think this trend has something to do with the failure of anyone else to offer convincing theories of pathogenesis, development, treatment, and cure, or a coherent theory of how the mind works. Neuropharmacology, neuropsychologies, relational psychology, self psychology, and CBT all were celebrated when first introduced, but none have fulfilled their early promise.

145

Changing Notions of the Feminine: Confronting Psychoanalysts' Prejudices. Edited by Margarita Cereijido. New York: Routledge, 2019, xiv + 135 pp., $155.00 hardcover, $44.95 paperback.

Hattie Myers

Hattie Myers

315 Riverside Drive, 13D
New York, NY 10025-6311
Email: hatbmyers@gmail.com

Hattie Myers   Training and Supervising Analyst, Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research (IPTAR); Founder and Editor-in-Chief, ROOM: A Sketchbook for Analytic Action

URL: http://www.analyticroom.com

The invitation to review “this slender volume,” Changing Notions of the Feminine: Confronting Psychoanalysts' Prejudices, the latest book in the Routledge series Psychoanalysis and Women, should really have come with a trigger warning. The seductive word “slender” suggested to me that the book would be a lovely, elegant, and perhaps even “easy” read. Hardly.

Writing can be a dangerous act of exposure but perhaps never more so than when the subject pertains to what women want and who women are. Staying close to the book's titular promise, the ten seasoned psychoanalytic contributors, hailing from Europe, North America, and Latin America, are careful to suggest that their particular experiences with female patients should not be taken as emblematic of the whole. But against the background of widespread societal change that has surrounded the concept of the feminine, the reflections that these analysts offer about changes they have noticed in themselves and in their female patients over a lifetime of practice can't help but point to changes in psychoanalysis—about gender and, more generally, about sexuality.

Hans-Georg Gadamer (1960), perhaps more than any other philosopher of the twentieth century, had a great deal to say about both truth and prejudice. Gadamer's particularly postmodern prejudice was that “truth” is not something that can be affirmed relative to a set of criteria but rather is an event or experience in which we find ourselves changed. In a comprehensive introduction, this collection's editor, Margarita Cereijido, tells us that the concept of prejudice alludes to a judgment that precedes experience—corresponding to Kant's a priori. By organizing and discriminating among “facts” we assess and understand the world. But Cereijido, like Gadamer, goes beyond enlightenment notions that considered prejudice an impediment to true knowledge. Gadamer revealed how prejudices are, hermeneutically speaking, not simply inescapable but fundamentally necessary to the ongoing production of understanding. Cereijido adds a psychoanalytic spin to this postmodern idea.

Jeffrey Ochsner

Jeffrey Ochsner

Box 355720
Seattle, WA 98195-5720
Email: jochsner@uw.edu

Jeffrey Ochsner   Professor, Department of Architecture, University of Washington.

Although Sigmund Freud often presented psychoanalysis as a “discovery,” its extension by later generations of analysts has demonstrated that the territory of the Freudian unconscious, focused as it was on the triangular oedipal drama, was a construction of Freud's own making. The field's preoedipal and relational turns—as well as feminist, gay/lesbian, and other critiques—have significantly enriched and extended the breadth and depth of psychoanalytic practice and theory. In recent years, the emergent field of Freud studies has increased our understanding of the personal travails and cultural context that shaped Freud's journey and molded his thinking. To these explorations, Diane O'Donoghue has now added On Dangerous Ground: Freud's Visual Cultures of the Unconscious

Confronting Bias in Psychoanalytic Theory

Sexuality and Gender Now: Moving beyond Heteronormativity. By Leezah Hertzmann and Julia Newbigin. New York: Routledge, 2019, xx + 336 pp., $140.00 hardcover, $39.95 paperback.

David Pauley

David Pauley

401 Pacific Street, Suite B1
Brooklyn, NY 11217
Email: davidpauleylcsw@gmail.com

David Pauley   Training and Supervising Analyst and faculty, Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy Study Center, New York.

Reading the collection Sexuality and Gender Now: Moving Beyond Heteronormativity, edited by the London-based psychoanalysts Leezah Hertzmann and Juliet Newbigin, I found myself toggling between two distinct historical moments: the present day, including the seminar on transference and countertransference I was teaching, and the winter of 1993, when as a graduate student I attended “Perspectives on Homosexuality: An Open Dialogue” at the NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, one of the first gatherings to confront our profession's sweeping history of heterosexism.1 Though I was scarcely aware of it at the time, that 1993 symposium occurred at a significant moment: just a few years after the American Psychoanalytic Association (APsaA), under threat of litigation, agreed to admit homosexual candidates to training (see Drescher 2008); and a half-decade before APsaA would fully renounce its view of homosexuality as pathological, a step that other major U.S. mental health organizations had taken in the 1970s.

A similar sense of history pervades Hertzmann and Newbigin's volume. Although the editors position the book, modestly, as “a training text for students as well as a resource for practicing clinicians” (p. 5), this mostly British collection (four of its eleven contributors are American) is in fact much more than that.

1That conference's proceedings were published as Disorienting Sexuality: Psychoanalytic Reappraisals of Sexual Identities (Domenici and Lesser 1995).301

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and the Pandemic: Editor's Introduction

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

Email: mdwilsonmd@berkeley.edu

Mitchell Wilson  

Tarrou had some comments on the sermon by Paneloux: “I can understand that type of fervor and find it not displeasing. At the beginning of a pestilence and when it ends, there's always a propensity for rhetoric. In the first case, habits have not yet been lost; in the second, they're returning. It is in the thick of a calamity that one gets hardened to the truth—-in other words, to silence. So let's wait.”

—Albert Camus, The Plague

As Camus suggests, in times of rapid or traumatic social change there is always a risk of speaking too soon because the “propensity for rhetoric” is great. At JAPA, with the world in the thick of a pandemic, we decided to take that risk and not stay silent. Back in late March we sent out a call for writing that describes aspects of what is happening for psychoanalytically oriented clinicians and their patients in the time of Covid-19. For many of us, chronological time, strangely, seems to have both sped up and slowed way down, as the days all roll mournfully into one another. The choreography of movement and talk that gave sense and color to a typical workday has suddenly stopped. Thankfully, out of this collective stasis and trauma have emerged writings of unusual vitality and poignancy that we have culled for this section of JAPA. We received any number of remarkable documents from a variety of practitioners, working in a variety of settings, who have given serious thought to their role and experience as the pandemic takes its course.

It is tempting to sing the praises of psychoanalysis and psychoanalysts in times like these—-an unfortunate example, perhaps, of not heeding Camus's caution regarding our propensity for rhetoric.

433

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Doubters and Believers in the Time of the Pandemic

Richard Tuch

Richard Tuch

1800 Fairburn Avenue, Suite 206
Los Angeles, CA 90025-6230
Email: rtuch@aol.com

Richard Tuch  

Until now, the great divide separating we, the people, had been defined chiefly by where one stood regarding our current president. But as the coronavirus raced its way cross country, a new divide opened up between “doubters,” who've insisted the viral threat isn't nearly as bad as some make it seem, and “believers,” who are quaking in their boots, fearing the end is near. While these two ends of the human response spectrum—the fearless and the fearful—have coexisted for eons, the extremity of this outbreak has widened the gap, compounding the challenges we collectively face as a nation.

Some disbelievers consider the protective measures put in place (if not in every place) draconian, extreme, and unnecessary. After all, they complain, most who become infected get only somewhat sick, so how can one justify bringing everyday life and the economy to a halt? Such attitudes terrify believers who do the math: if one careless freedom-lover gets infected, over the next month a hundred more Americans will become infected and two will die. Is one person's right to freely mingle more precious than another's right to life? Those who buy the grave projections offered by epidemiologists deeply distrust disbelievers, who, they fear, might blithely “party on”—selfishly placing others at risk by failing to follow the recommended precautions. Shaming such disbelieving noncompliers has become commonplace—whether they are called out publicly or are privately held in contempt in the confines of one's mind. And so the divide widens.

Former Dean of Training, New Center for Psychoanalysis, Los Angeles; Training and Supervising Analyst, New Center for Psychoanalysis and the Psychoanalytic Center of California; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, David Geffen School of Medicine at UCLA.

Submitted for publication April 23, 2020.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Masochism: A Resident's Conflict between Sacrifice and Self-Preservation during the Covid-19 Pandemic

Gali Hashmonay

Gali Hashmonay

450 Clarkson Avenue
Brooklyn, NY 11203
Email: ghashmonay@gmail.com

Gali Hashmonay  

As a psychiatry resident working in New York City, I first saw the signs of Covid-19 in an outpatient setting. Before safety guidelines had been established, a homeless patient of mine walked in for an unplanned appointment. Hearing him coughing in the waiting room, I knew that neither of us would have a mask to wear, their being in short supply at the time. Ignoring the fact that given his living conditions and symptoms he was high risk for Covid-19, I brought him into my claustrophobic office for a session. As I heard his heart-rending description of his life in a virus-ridden shelter, I realized a little too late that I was greatly exposing myself. I questioned the obligation I had felt to see him without first taking proper precautions to protect my health. As events continued to accelerate in our hospital, I learned I was not the only resident too quick to compromise our safety. Alarmingly, it had become the status quo. As a therapy provider to other medical residents, I was gritting my teeth through one worrisome anecdote after another. My fellow residents and I were rushing into close patient contact with no protective equipment, eager to help and thinking little of our own needs. While this was undoubtedly noble and necessary in emergency situations, at other times our haste to enter a risk-filled room seemed to be coming more from an internal demand rather than from objective clinical urgency. On top of it all, we were guiltily declaring that we wanted to do even more.

Resident in Psychiatry, SUNY Downstate Medical Center.

Submitted for publication April 21, 2020.

471

From the Editors

Anne Adelman , Jennifer Stuart and Rachel Widawsky

Rachel Widawsky

Email: anne.adelmanphd@gmail.com

jennifer.stuart.phd@gmail.com

rsboue@gmail.com

Rachel Widawsky  

Profile: Joseph Lichtenberg

In this issue of the Review of Books, we are pleased to present a new essay format that we hope you will enjoy: a young colleague's discerning reading and personal profile of an influential analytic author looking back on his long life and career. In an essay focusing on two of Joseph Lichtenberg's recent works, reviewer Sameer Khan weaves observations from a guided tour of Lichtenberg's extensive photography collection with an exploration of his lifelong contribution to psychoanalytic thought: an expansion of Kohut's self psychology into a theory of “motivational systems.” As Khan notes, Lichtenberg's work often “shift[s] emphasis away from pathology and … toward shoring up what goes right in development … toward the ways human beings adapt their energies to grow, forge connections, and generate meaning.” Through Khan's interaction with Lichtenberg, we see the inextricable link of the theorizer's personhood with his particular contribution to psychoanalytic thought (a link no less essential for any living analytic theorist than it was for Freud, whose laser focus on the oedipal surely reflected matters of deep personal import to him!). We also see what happens when a young clinician confronts a clinical/theoretical perspective that is largely new to him and works to absorb it into his developing understanding of our field.

Psychoanalysis and the Visual Arts

From Khan's essay on Lichtenberg we move to Sandra Hershberg's review of work by another psychoanalyst whose immersion in the visual arts informs her contributions to our literature. Laurie Wilson is both a trained art historian and a practicing clinical psychoanalyst, known for her earlier biography of the sculptor Alberto Giacometti. Her most recent book is another exquisite work of psychoanalytic biography—this one, of the groundbreaking twentieth-century artist Louise Nevelson.

261

Childhood and Adolescence

Childhood beyond Pathology: A Psychoanalytic Study of Development and Diagnosis. By Lisa Farley. Albany: SUNY Press, 2018, x + 186 pp., $95.00 hardcover, $31.95 paperback.

Timothy Rice

Timothy Rice

1111 Amsterdam Avenue, 5 West
New York, NY 10025
Email: timothy.rice@mountsinai.org

Timothy Rice   Associate Professor of Psychiatry, Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai; Director for Medical Student Education in Psychiatry and Chief, Mount Sinai Health System's Child and Adolescent Inpatient Psychiatry Service, Mount Sinai St. Luke's-West Side.

Lisa Farley, Associate Professor of Education at York University in Toronto, has written an engaging volume from a psychoanalytically infused reconceptualist perspective on child development and psychopathology. A nuanced reading of Childhood beyond Pathology offers much to the psychoanalytically minded reader, especially those unfamiliar with this field of contemporary childhood educational study.

Following a short, thoughtfully written introduction that defines childhood as “neither a natural stage of development, nor a neutral category, but rather a placeholder for political struggles, philosophical ideals, and social anxieties” (p. 1), Childhood beyond Pathology engages in its method: Five disempowered children from the areas of fiction, recent sociopolitical events, and the psychoanalytic literature are tapped chapter by chapter in order “to open new conceptualizations of the child who oversteps or is cast outside of dominant trajectories circumscribing growth, learning, language, and being itself ” (p. 4). In this manner Childhood beyond Pathology studies development through disenfranchised figures in order to subversively reinscribe conceptualizations of the normal, natural, and universal as fabricated constructs that serve primarily as containers of oppressive power relations.

Despite the author's tendency to uncritically champion this perspective, Childhood beyond Pathology inarguably advances our discourse on child development, as a consideration of its chapters will demonstrate.

Chapter 1 summons the “replacement child,” a child born to a parent after another child has died, through an analysis of Ann-Marie MacDonald's 2014 novel, Adult Onset. The analysis places Freud's “Morning and Melancholia” (1917)

521

Sameer Khan

Sameer Khan

280 Madison Avenue, Suite 506

Sameer Khan   Senior psychoanalytic candidate, Psychoanalytic Association of New York (formerly known as the Institute for Psychoanalytic Education), affiliated with the NYU School of Medicine.

“Come on in, have a look around,” says Joe Lichtenberg as he answers the door to his home in Bethesda, Maryland. “There's much to look at!”

He's not wrong. The entryway splits off into two staircases, one leading up to a landing, and the other, shorter, running off into sunken drawing and dining rooms, and the kitchen. So the guest is immediately placed at three levels—hall at ground, landing above, living space below—and flanked at all three by museum-worthy photographic prints. They are large, black and white, and matted on white to seem even larger, gathered as closely together as wall space and good taste will allow. Many of the photographs are stunningly rendered land- or seascapes; a few feature people and events. Each is clearly by a prominent artist; each captures light and texture with a breathtaking clarity that calls out for closer study. The invitation for the guest to wander is immediate.

One photo in particular catches my eye as I follow Dr. Lichtenberg down the stairs to his living room: an 8 × 10 gelatin silver print by Sally Mann, titled “At My Mother's House.” The photo shows a living room darkened—or dimmed, rather—by sheer white curtains that give the afternoon light filtering through them a granular, almost mote-like quality.

267

The Psychoanalyst as Memoirist

The Known, the Secret, the Forgotten: A Memoir. By Joan Wheelis. New York: Norton, 2019, xii + 164 pp., $24.95 hardcover.

Salman Akhtar

Salman Akhtar

833 Chestnut East 210-C
Philadelphia, PA 19107
Email: salman.akhtar@jefferson.edu

Salman Akhtar   Professor of Psychiatry, Jefferson Medical College; Training and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Center of Philadelphia.

All sorts of questions arise in my mind as I begin writing the review of this elegant and engaging memoir by the Boston-based psychoanalyst Joan Wheelis. The cacophony of inquiry puts a lid on the emotions that have become liquid inside me. I feel the tenderness of an older brother, the vaguely remorseful glee of a voyeur (I know the author and her analyst parents), the admiration of a demanding reader, and the envy of a proud writer. More powerfully, I experience the empathic resonance of a fellow human being with the fact that we—all of us—lead lives of pleasure and pain, certainty and doubt, rupture and continuity, reality and imagination. The questions that crowd my mind may be intended to divert attention from this sentimental chaos, but that does not diminish their significance.

Prominent among these questions are the following: Should I be “scientific” in my approach to the book? Should my review be replete with page numbers of cited passages, references to pertinent psychoanalytic literature, and “translations” of the author's observations into psychoanalytic jargon? Or, like someone seeing the Taj Mahal for the first time, should I allow rapture to take over, to become mesmerized and haunted by the tales, true and apocryphal, about the monument and its maker? Another line of inquiry presses me to ask if a modicum of self-disclosure (regarding my parents, my childhood, my personal life) will add to or detract from my giving the reader of this review a full sense of Wheelis's highly personal account? What should I do with this literary countertransference? And, finally, there is the big question: How do I avoid the risk of “wild analysis” here?

745

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Falling Out of the World—and the Longing for Home

Alfred Margulies

Alfred Margulies

1493 Cambridge Street
Cambridge, MA 02139-1099
Email: alfred_margulies@hms.harvard.edu

Alfred Margulies  

catastrophe, pandemic, the uncanny, groundlessness, fantasy of home, return of the repressed, après-coup, entangled time, witnessing, being-in-the-world

The center does not hold. The ground gives way, and we are lost and falling. The ancients knew about the sudden overturning of the order of things: “Catastrophe” derives from the Greek, from “down” and “turn over.” We lose the world we took for granted, and the familiar turns strange. “Familiar”—a word derived from family.

At the dawn of Western literature, Homer sang of Odysseus longing to get back to family, to home, to Penelope. For twenty years that clever warrior weeps on strange shores. The Bible, too, begins with the loss of Eden, that perfect garden-home of innocence, before awareness, before knowledge. Home, then, is not merely something to be found in space: home is a state of being; a longing steeped in our earliest fantasies, of touch and bodily presence and love. And of course, because this longing is saturated in fantasies, there may never have been such a home, but rather a memory of a fantasy. Perhaps this fantasy is a primal illusion born in our earliest pre-aware moments on being thrust out into a world we did not choose. Home is a longing for something deep and ineffable. As Bob Dylan (1985) puts it, in Scorsese's PBS documentary No Direction Home, “I was born very far from where I am supposed to be, and so I'm on my way home.” Home is a longing for the world to be set right. Today this is our collective longing; yet we all long in our own way.

Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; faculty, Harvard Medical School, Department of Psychiatry, the Cambridge Health Alliance.

Parts of this paper were presented to the plenary panel, “Transformations of Psychoanalytic Experience and Practice in the Covid-19 Era,” American Psychoanalytic Association Virtual Annual Meeting, June 19, 2020.

responseSet.title: Spatially Distant but Emotionally Close: A Personal Reflection on Psychoanalytic Distance Training
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Spatially Distant but Emotionally Close: A Personal Reflection on Psychoanalytic Distance Training

Nahaleh Moshtagh

Nahaleh Moshtagh

9 Haghparast Alley, Sarafraz Avenue, Shahid Beheshti Street
Tehran
IRAN
Email: nahaleh.moshtagh@gmail.com

Nahaleh Moshtagh  

distance training, emotional communication

Psychoanalytic training in countries with no accredited training program or official link to the international psychoanalytic community is possible only through technology. Serious controversies surround the legitimacy of distance training, based primarily on doubts about the efficacy of distance analysis. Curiously, in a discipline that prides itself on being a “talking cure,” an emphasis on the embodied presence of the analytic couple emerges in the literature over and over again. In fact, though, it's through the use of language—and not through sight—that we achieve the aim of analysis. The information the analyst gathers from bodily presence is important, but even then the analyst's job is to hear the patient's unconscious communications. If an analyst, for whatever reason, is unable to hear the patient's voice and unconscious communications, the analysis is bound to fail whether the patient is in the room or on the screen/phone. Being spatially distant poses no contradictions to psychoanalysis, as long as both parties—patient and analyst, supervisee and supervisor, candidate and instructor—are willing to listen, hear, and be heard emotionally.

[[RunningHead]]

Law, Gender, and the Liberal Project in American Psychoanalytic Thought

Feminine Law: Freud, Free Speech, and the Voice of Desire. By Jill Gentile with Michael Macrone. London: Karnac Books, 2016, xxxviii + 290 pp., $149.95 hardcover, $46.95 paperback.Law and the Unconscious: A Psychoanalytic Perspective. By Anne C. Dailey. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2017, xxii + 283 pp., $40.00 hardcover.Law, Psychoanalysis, Society: Taking the Unconscious Seriously. By Maria Aristodemou. New York: Routledge, 2014, x + 179 pp., $150.00 hardcover, $55.95 paperback.

Nomi Stolzenberg

Nomi Stolzenberg

699 West Exposition Boulevard, Room 476
University of Southern California Gould School of Law
Los Angeles, CA 90089
Email: nstolzenberg@law.usc.edu

Nomi Stolzenberg   Stolzenberg, Professor of Law and Nathan and Lilly Shapell Chair in Law, University of Southern California Gould School of Law; co-founder and co-director, USC Center for Law, History, and Culture; member of the advisory board, Max Planck Institute for the Study of Religious and Ethnic Diversity.

Jill Gentile's Feminine Law: Freud, Free Speech, and the Voice of Desire is one of three books to have come out in recent years that yoke law and psychoanalysis to generate new understandings of the modern subject and the modern state. Beyond their shared methodological commitment to combining psychoanalytic and legal theory, these three books have a number of other other things in common—not least, a critical perspective on gender and a keen awareness of the masculine bias present in both psychoanalysis and law. The first of these books, Law, Psychoanalysis, Society: Taking the Unconscious Seriously (2014), was written by Maria Aristodemou, a professor at London's Birkbeck School of Law. Aristodemou's previous book, Law and Literature: Journeys from Her to Eternity (2000), argued that women's reproductive and nurturing capacities pose a threat to masculinity, which is duplicated in the challenge posed by literature to law. Her latest book renounces her earlier aspiration to use literature (coded as emotional and female) to overcome the “long exclusion of the feminine” from the “empire” of law (coded as rational and male). Compared to its predecessor, it is less explicitly concerned with gender.

503

Personal Meaning: Anthropology, Psychoanalysis, and “Individuology”

The Psychoanalytic Ear and the Sociological Eye: Toward an American Independent Tradition. By Nancy J. Chodorow. New York: Routledge, 2020, xxvi + 290 pp., $44.95 paperback.Arguing Sainthood: Modernity, Psychoanalysis, and Islam. By Katherine Pratt Ewing. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 2018, viii + 419 pp., $120.00 hardcover, $37.50 pqperback.

Robert Paul

Robert Paul

5565 Bahia Mar Circle
Stone Mountain, GA 30087
Email: rpaul@emory.edu

Robert Paul   Charles Howard Candler Professor of Anthropology and Interdisciplinary Studies, and Adjunct Professor, Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences, Emory University; Training and Supervising Analyst, Emory University Psychoanalytic Institute.

My intention in this essay is to present to a psychoanalytic readership some important books in the field of anthropology that exemplify the kind of research Nancy Chodorow advocates with her newly coined term individuology. Chodorow has long argued for placing psychoanalysis in an interdisciplinary context, specifically one that stresses the importance of the cultural context of clinical theory and practice. While many analysts have long taken an interest in the humanities and the arts, and more recently in neuroscience, Chodorow urges us to focus as well on the adjacent social sciences, sociology and particularly cultural anthropology. In a time when psychoanalysts are taking more and more interest in seeing psychoanalysis in its social and political dimensions, Chodorow's book, together with the three ethnographic monographs under review here, make valuable contributions to furthering and enriching that endeavor.

721

Psychoanalysis and Fashion

Psychoanalysis in Fashion. Edited by Anita Weinreb Katz and Arlene Kramer Richards. New York: IPBooks, 2018, xii + 326 pp., $35.00 paperback.

Carola Chase

Carola Chase

680 West End Avenue, Suite 1F
New York, NY 10025
Email: cfbchase@gmail.com

Carola Chase   Member, Contemporary Freudian Society; Program Director, Service Program for Older People (SHOP).

Psychoanalysis in Fashion explores the subject of clothes, body ornamentation, and fashion from psychoanalytic, interpersonal, and cultural perspectives. The insights presented highlight the relevance of fashion for investigation in clinical work. Readers who might dismiss this book because of its apparently light, if charming, topic—this is a book about fashion, after all, even the cover a bright collage of fashion illustrations—should reconsider. Psychoanalysis in Fashion approaches the subject of fashion seriously, academically, but not without humor.

The book's editors, Anita Weinreb Katz and Arlene Kramer Richards, one of whom is referred to by a patient as “fashionista analysta” (an alternative title for this book?) have assembled an eclectic array of contributors from the fields of psychoanalysis, fashion, and academia to address a wide selection of issues related to fashion. Topics include shopping compulsions, clothes as fetish, the multiple conscious and unconscious meanings of jewelry, shopping and display, female cross-dressing, and the varied uses and significance of tattoos. Consistent with the aesthetic, visual nature of fashion, illustrations—of tattoos, hairstyles, Chinese handwork, and even the editors' ancestors (dressed to the nines)—embellish, and enhance, the book.

Psychoanalysis in Fashion is divided into seven parts, beginning with personal considerations, musings, by Katz and Richards about their interest in fashion and in writing this book. Both grew up enjoying the experience of shopping and of being “consumers” of fashion (p. 6). Later, as psychoanalysts, their work motivated each to understand the meanings, fantasies, emotions, and significance, conscious and unconscious, of fashion in their patients' lives.

751

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Learning to Write

Arlene Richards

Arlene Richards

200 East 89th Street
New York, NY 10128-4308
Email: arlenerichards89@gmail.com

Arlene Richards  

I became a bookish child in several steps. At six I went to live with my parents. Everyone in my home read. My mother read nursery rhymes to me while I ate in order to get me to enjoy eating. My mother read novels herself to pass the time when she was at home with her child. My father read Book of the Month Club selections.

But my respect for reading and writing began much earlier. I was brought up mainly by my grandfather until the age of six. He would write at the dining room table on Sunday mornings. Everyone was hushed while he was doing this important work. The importance of writing and reading were impressed on me indelibly. Only much later I learned that he was addressing envelopes for the immigrant society of which he was the secretary.

When my mother read those nursery rhymes to me she was coaxing me to eat the simple, bland American food she cooked, though I longed for my grandmother's spicy, salty complex Middle European cooking. What I couldn't have known is that my mother was teaching me English, and at the same time was teaching me how to read. She was also giving me a taste for poetry.

So her motives were complex even while her cooking was not.

I was a lonely child, often left to my own devices when my mother was in bed trying not to have a miscarriage. I longed for the grandparents who were my emotional home. I read stories about other little girls. Heidi by Johanna Spyri was my favorite. In that story a little girl leaves unhealthy city life to go live with a grandfather on a mountain. There she meets a wild little boy, spends lots of time outdoors, eats toasted cheese, and grows healthy herself. The story fit my dream of going back to be with my grandfather, having other kids to play with, and being healthy.

Training and Supervising Analyst, Contemporary Freudian Society.

[[RunningHead]]

Can You See Me?

Lynne Zeavin

Lynne Zeavin

80 University Place, Fifth Floor
New York, NY 10003
Email: drlynne.zeavin@gmail.com

Lynne Zeavin  

A part of writing is overcoming the difficulty of writing. This is perhaps always true, but it is even more so now, as the coronavirus reality thickens and spreads, its sinister shadow ever more present. The world is sick, I think to myself. The exhortations to not return to normal seem right, that facing what normal has been is to realize the spell we have all been under, of course to varying degrees, for as long as I can remember. And yet this leaves us suspended, up in the air, without points of orientation: we can't know when, how, where.

We are without some of our usual external supports, those markers of identity that come from without and help shape what's within.

In my own psychoanalytic work, a concept that is useful to me is that of the good internal object. For Melanie Klein, the good internal object is hard won through facing our hatred and wishes to do harm to those we love. The good object inside is different from idealization, though it requires idealization early on to establish itself. Idealization works to keep out of awareness destructive urges and feelings, the persecutory anxieties and fantasies that interfere with the establishment of the good internal object early on. If we get to the point of being able to recognize that the object we need is also the object we wish in fantasy to destroy, if that object can tolerate our hatred, without too much vengeance or collapse, we have a chance of having inside something like a good object that is sustaining, that can provide a point of orientation.

The other day I was consulting (on Zoom, of course) with a group of candidates. One of them presented some work I will discuss here.

The candidate described her patient, who, like many patients, is having difficulty making the transition to working in this new mode. A., a woman in her early thirties, began the first session of the week saying,

[[RunningHead]]

Becoming of use as an Analyst: Imagining Something That was Never There Before

Fred Griffin

Fred Griffin

8235 Douglas Avenue, Suite 822
Dallas, TX 75225
Email: fredgriffinmd@gmail.com

Fred Griffin  

countertransference, creativity, holding environment, intersubjectivity, object relations, transference, development

Every analysis that becomes generative must be an original experience, in both senses of the word: an experience that reaches into the origins of the emotional life of the analysand and one that creates something entirely new. If the analysis goes well enough, these efforts lead the analysand to feel the oldness and newness to be an integrated whole. Analysands who have experienced an absence of intimate emotional connection early in life especially require that new psychological capacity be developed from their germinal potential as a means by which the psychoanalytic process is brought to life. In such cases, both analyst and analysand are required to find ways to expand their receptivity to sensory experience and to cultivate their imaginative capacities in a manner that make emotional growth possible. Through this process words become embodied and better able to articulate the analysand's self-experience and to create the experience of reciprocity with the analyst and with others in ways never before experienced.

[[RunningHead]]

Ce que le nazisme a fait à la psychanalyse (What Nazism Has Done to Psychoanalysis). By Laurence Kahn. Paris: PUF, 2018, 259 pp., €14,00 paperback.

Rachel Widawsky

Rachel Widawsky

88 Lexington Avenue, Apt. 1105
New York, NY 10016
Email: rsboue@gmail.com

Rachel Widawsky   JAPA Foreign Book Review Editor.

In spite of the importance of Laurence Kahn's What Nazism Has Done to Psychoanalysis,1 I hesitated to review it at a time when the credentials of psychoanalysis are already undermined. But Kahn's arduous undertaking—to face an unpleasant truth, the destructive impact of Nazism on psychoanalysis—prompted me to review her book, a work I think should become an historical reference. As a philosopher, a polyglot philologist, and a psychoanalyst, Kahn goes beyond psychoanalytic theory and clinical questions. Her book is a history of the destruction wrought by Nazis on postwar and contemporary psychoanalysis. Beyond the autos-da-fé2 of Freud's books, the exile of psychoanalysts, and the problematic history of the Göring Institute (founded by Herman Göring's cousin and directed by Carl Jung), Kahn's intention is to explain theoretical distortions of psychoanalytic theory in response to Nazi ideology.

The first damage Kahn identifies is the corruption of the concept of Führer, which began in 1934—nine years after the publication of Mein Kampf, and five years before Freud published Moses and Monotheism. Kahn underscores that by the time Freud (1939) called Moses a Führer, the term was already perverted from its ordinary meaning by Nazi ideology. It is inconceivable that Freud was not aware of the political meaning of the word while he was writing Moses and Monotheism. Kahn considers the intended meaning of Freud's last book in that historical context and suggests that the urgent message of Moses and Monotheism for Freud was to restate, after Totem and Taboo (1912-1913) and Group Psychology and the Analysis of the Ego (1921), that the role of the “great leader,” in religions and societies, is a predicate for an ethical community coalesced around the prohibition of murder. Here one can recognize Freud's early neurotic narrative applied to the origin of monotheistic religions, as well

1If psychoanalysis has a public voice in French media, it is usually solicited around controversies or social events. Kahn's arduous book on the history of psychoanalysis through the lens of Nazism was unanimously praised by the popular press and radio shows as a reference for understanding the epistemological situation of psychoanalysis today.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editor's Note

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

Email: mdwmd57@icloud.com

Mitchell Wilson  

As everyone is well aware, we are in the midst of an unfolding pandemic. Each of us is affected, and though we have all had to make significant (perhaps lasting) changes in our personal and professional lives, in truth, as I write this the outcomes for each and every one of us are changing and our experiences vary. Illness, death, vigilance, complacency, boredom—these are some of the facts of life for many of us as citizens. As clinicians and psychoanalysts, we have other responsibilities as well. In future issues of JAPA we will feature writing on Covid-19 and clinical practice from a variety of settings (both in and beyond the United States), including, especially, the inevitable reframings of therapeutic purpose and the values that underwrite what it is we do and why we do it.

In this issue of JAPA we focus on trends and innovations in psychoanalytic education. Typically, recent writing on educational trends emphasizes the “market forces” that drive them (e.g., the declining number of candidates). This suggests that in the absence of such forces we would continue to teach psychoanalysis in the way we always have. Put in these terms, it is fairly easy to see that this emphasis on economic realities can obscure a far deeper question: Is psychoanalytic knowledge a universal, timeless entity that floats above and independent of the social surround and the political moment? Clearly it does not. We are all of us well aware of the ways in which the psychoanalytic view of the human person is directly affected by specific beliefs and prejudices alive within the culture. Our task is to be alert to these interactive effects, as well as to changes in the kinds of suffering we encounter in our clinical settings. Only in this way—what I think of as a recursive dialectical awareness of these interactions—can we meaningfully ask the deeper and more enduring questions about psychoanalytic values, beliefs, and assertions of fact in relation to how we educate candidates and the larger community of clinicians.

[[RunningHead]]

Exception as Cure

Sergio Benvenuto

Sergio Benvenuto

via San Martino dalla Battaglia 44
Roma 00185
ITALY
Email: eu.jou.psy@gmail.com

Sergio Benvenuto  

I have in treatment several people who went through long periods of self-quarantine, long before the coronavirus crisis: for months, sometimes years, they never left home. If they lived alone, they would go out only for groceries or to go to the post office. If they had a job, they gave it up; if they were students, they interrupted their studies. (Some applied for universal basic income payments, which in my opinion encouraged their syndrome.) It's a syndrome without a name—it may be referred to as agoraphobia, but in fact these people are not generally afraid of open spaces; they're afraid of others, of the crowd around them, pushing and shoving. I would call them claustrophiles, stealing the term from my mentor in psychoanalysis, Elvio Fachinelli, author of the 1983 book Claustrofilia.

Years ago, a now mature woman was afraid of contracting AIDS on buses by holding on to the hanging straps.

Others stay at home out of hypochondria: they feel besieged by death and espy every sensation or physiological symptom as the signal of some fatal pathology. One patient, who spent months locked at home alone, would collect his urine in small transparent pots, line them up on a long shelf, and contemplate them for hours, studying any changes in color and trying to interpret what terrible illnesses these changes might be signaling.

Others—labeled seriously obsessive-compulsive subjects—are afraid of others (and of objects, insofar as they are shared with others) as ever present sources of infection. When they do go out, they wear gloves and try to prevent anyone touching them. They can by no means use the bathroom in other people's homes or, of course, in public places. In some cases they always stand, even at work, to avoid any contact with a chair.

Researcher, National Council for Scientific Research (CNR), Rome; President, Institute for Advanced Studies in Psychoanalysis (ISAP), Rome; Editor, European Journal of Psychoanalysis; Editorial Board, American Imago.

From the Editors

Anne Adelman , Jennifer Stuart and Rachel Boué-Widawsky

Rachel Boué-Widawsky

Email: anne.adelmanphd@gmail.com

jennifer,stuart.phd@gmail.com

rsboue@gmail.com

Rachel Boué-Widawsky  

Book Essay: Psychoanalysis and the Law

We open this issue of JAPA Review of Books with an essay by Nomi Stolzenberg, a legal scholar whose discerning reading of Freud, Winnicott, and Lacan informs a rich body of work at the juncture of law with political theory, culture, and religion. Stolzenberg discusses two books by fellow legal scholars—Maria Aristodemou's Law, Psychoanalysis, and Society: Taking the Unconscious Seriously and Anne Dailey's Law and the Unconscious: A Psychoanalytic Perspective—and one by a psychoanalyst, Jill Gentile's Feminine Law: Freud, Free Speech, and the Voice of Desire. In an informative essay titled “Law, Gender, and the Liberal Project in American Psychoanalytic Thought,” Stolzenberg notes that all three works share “a critical perspective on gender and a keen awareness of the masculine bias present in both psychoanalysis and law.” However, they diverge in their appeals to different portions of the psychoanalytic literature, and—commensurately—in their takes on the gendered nature of legal theory. Aristodemou draws from Lacan, and consequently figures law as a “phallic” system in which forbidden desire for incestuous union with the mother is repressed. In contrast, Dailey's view of legal authority rests on Winnicott's conception of “maternal function.” Gentile's goal, writes Stolzenberg, is to construct a model of “feminine law” that combines “the French tradition of Lacan and Kristeva and the British and American traditions of object relations theory and ego psychology.” Stolzenberg questions whether this combination can succeed in serving Gentile's goal of creating a system of law that “liberate[s]” and “democratize[s] desire.” But she applauds the effort to meld Lacanian and Winnicottian thought about language, perception, infant development, and maternal and paternal authority to construct a model of law based on traditionally female functions. And she notes the timely nature of Gentile's work: “as the specter of illiberal democracy returns to Europe and America,” she writes, “so, too, psychoanalysis is returning to help us diagnose our political maladies and, perhaps, to offer a cure.

[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Essig

Nahaleh Moshtagh

Nahaleh Moshtagh

122 South Michigan Avenue
Chicago, IL 60603-6184
Email: nahaleh.moshtagh@gmail.com

Nahaleh Moshtagh  

In his letter to JAPA, Todd Essig makes some comments to “correct the record” of what he calls my “serious misrepresentation” of his work, as well as “a pair of counterproductive assumptions” in my recent paper (Moshtagh 2020, JAPA 68/2).

Essig starts his letter by mocking the paper as “Nahaleh Moshtagh's tale of an heroic journey to receive psychoanalytic training” and ends with the admonition “We should do better,” leaving me to wonder if there is something wrong with sharing one's tale in a psychoanalytic journal. In fact, psychoanalysis was the fruit of Freud's desire to listen to tales and hear their communications. Ever since, psychoanalysis has called us to open our horizons and go beyond ourselves to bear witness to another human being's journey, in our consulting room, on the screen, or in texts. Was my journey heroic? It definitely feels like that to me. Adam Phillips, in Becoming Freud: The Making of Psychoanalysis (2014), suggests that to Freud heroes are “men who defined their moment, not men struggling to assimilate to their societies; … a man who would face, in a new way, the facts of his own life” (p. 5). In fact, that's exactly what makes all distance candidates heroes. As I emphasize in my paper, we are facing life in a new way, passionately, courageously, and committed to our shared cause. This leads me to my second point.

697

[[RunningHead]]

Plea for a Safe Place

The Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott: Volume 3. 1946-1951. Edited by Vincent Bonaminio and Peter Fabozzi. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2016.

Giuseppe Civitarese

Giuseppe Civitarese

Piazza A. Botta 1
27100 Pavia
ITALY
Email: gcivitarese@gmail.com

Giuseppe Civitarese   Training and Supervising Analyst, Italian Psychoanalytic Society; Editor, Revista di psicoanalisi.

“Plea for a safe place” is the title that immediately came to my mind for this brief comment on the third volume of Winnicott's Collected Works. Reading the book carefully, line by line, I realized its extraordinary unity. As a musical motif, the main theme appears and disappears in the texts written for various occasions: an essay to be published, a speech at a conference, a letter to a newspaper or a colleague, and so on. As in a symphonic score, this allows for an endless series of variations on the same motif, but each time it also seems new and surprising. It is as if Winnicott is concerned solely with the one thing he thinks is important, to the point of becoming obsessed with it. Whether he talks about antisocial children, real criminals, the trauma of birth, or hatred in the countertransference, the central point is always the home (“good environment” / “primary home experiences” / “child's own home” / “good start”) in which the psyche is born, what jeopardizes it, and what can be done when the damage has already been wrought.

It is necessary to bear in mind the social and historical context of these writings, which span the years 1946 to 1951, and thus date back to the period immediately following the Second World War. War often appears, page after page, not only as a memory of recent dramatic experiences but also as a negative figure (the “broken home” / “war situation” / “wartime” / “evacuation breakdown” / “unsettled homes”) of the safe place.

This essay is the third in a continuing series of reviews of the twelve volumes of D. W. Winnicott's Collected Works, general editors Lesley Caldwell and Helen Taylor Robinson. Guest Editors: Phillip Blumberg and Adrienne Harris.

Jill Gentile  

Time may change me

But you can't trace time

Strange fascinations fascinate me

…

Turn and face the strange

—David Bowie, Changes

The psychic atmosphere of everyday life is that of disturbing strangeness….

The most familiar places become foreign places.

—Bernard Chervet

It's not a question of vocabulary, it's a question of time.

—Dr. Rieux in Camus's The Plague

The core function of psychoanalysis is to confront the rips, gaps, and aporias in our experiences and discourses. We aim to turn confusion into a space for discovery; we aim to reckon with genuine alterity; we aim to question the known knowns. It is a process defined by its dismantling of received knowledge and customary experience, by its disruption of normalized circuits of expectancies and repetitions' compulsions, by its aims to reveal unpalatable, certainly conflicted, truths and desires. As soon as we incline to arrest change by imposing certainty—to

Faculty, NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

Kylie Svenson

Kylie Svenson

1902 Webster Street
San Francisco, CA 94115
Email: kyliesvensontherapy@gmail.com

Kylie Svenson  

dissociation, countertransference, transference, trauma, frame, Covid-19, teleanalysis

While we and our patients have many transferences to the modality of video or telephone therapy, these meanings are ultimately not intrinsic to the medium itself. Rather, they are symbolic meanings we make of the modality. This paper discusses the symbolic meanings that we attribute to teletherapy and the ways we may use these attributed meanings to dissociate the trauma of Covid-19. It also explores the clinical opportunities in identifying these dissociated countertransferential experiences and embodying them in our own and our patients' experiences.

Merav Rabinovich  

case study metasynthesis, qualitative research, RBC method, theoretical integration, psychology method, psychoanalytic research

The Relationships Between Categories (RBC) technique is a qualitative methodology for the metasynthesis of psychoanalytic case studies. By analyzing repetitive bilateral, trilateral, or quadrilateral relationships of transference themes, this methodology seeks to analyze case studies with existing theoretical concepts, thereby formulating a new theory. The proposed tool attempts to explore and validate hidden connections between different psychotherapy components, thus enhancing integration of various bodies of knowledge and decreasing the gap between practice and theory. This methodology is demonstrated here by research on transference case studies that connect transference to components of cognitive behavioral therapy.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

I Write to Find out what I am Thinking

Sheldon Bach

Sheldon Bach

Email: sheldonbach@gmail.com

Sheldon Bach  

I imagine that from an objective point of view the answer to “why I write” would be complicated and different for everyone, requiring an analytic biography to subsume the leading themes. But subjectively I have a clear and immediate response: I write in order to find out what I am thinking. Some psychoanalytic writers I know have a clear idea of what they want to communicate before they begin a paper, or even a set of mental or actual notes, perhaps even an outline. But for the most part I begin only with some feeling in mind, a clinical conundrum, an issue or clinical oddity that I have recently observed or one that has been rolling around in my head for some time, appearing and disappearing or even sometimes showing up in dreams. And so it is there but not quite there, waiting to be discovered or hatched, and the process of writing is, for me, simply my way of actualizing what has been going on by itself for some time.

This may sound like a disavowal of responsibility for one's thoughts, something not unknown to creative writers and poets and even to psychoanalysts. I recall that Freud seemed almost eager to attribute the dream book to Fliess, as if in some way an intermediary or muse might be necessary to shield the flame of inspiration from the work itself, or as if someone else should be blamed for the disorder, even plague, that he was bringing into the world. In our language, I may be talking about the transformation of primary process into secondary process, of unconscious or preconscious to conscious, of unformulated experience to formulated experience and also a transformation of binary thinking to a more modulated way of viewing experience as endlessly linked on a continuum.

That's a lot of jargon and theoretical lattice to describe the apparently simple act of turning a dream or fantasy into a story.

Teaching faculty and Clinical Consultant, NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychonalysis, Contemporary Freudian Track.

[[RunningHead]]

Emerging from Deadness: A Microanalysis of the Analytic Relationship

Ruth Graver

Ruth Graver

200 West 90th Street, Suite 1G
New York, NY 10024
Email: rlg22@cumc.columbia.edu

Ruth Graver  

attachment, development, unconscious, working through, intersubjectivity, enactment

Video microanalysis, a technique developed by infant researchers, is used to understand the withdrawal that developed between analyst and analysand when the latter resumed use of the couch after a period of sitting up. The case includes three excerpts of microprocess, accompanied by descriptions of content apart from explicit verbal material, content such as tone of voice, speech patterns, facial expression, and body movements, along with diagrams showing the second-by-second vocal rhythm coordination of analyst and analysand. Supervision using the video, as well as the analyst's viewing the video with the analysand in a modified use of video feedback, widened the pair's understanding of the determinants of their mutual participation in withdrawal and a feeling of deadness, thus freeing them from the repetition of an enactment. It is shown how (1) movement to the couch created an affectively heightened state that brought central psychodynamic aspects of the analysand's experience to the fore; (2) video microanalysis allowed access to previously unavailable content; and (3) understanding of unconscious, dynamically determined conflicts and defenses embedded in body movement, facial expression, speech tone, and rhythm patterns illuminated facets of the co-created relatedness between analysand and analyst.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reconsidering Leonardo Da Vinci and a Memory of His Childhood

Rajiv Gulati and David Pauley

David Pauley

401 Pacific Street, Suite B2
Brooklyn, NY 11217
Email: rajivgulatimd@gmail.com

401 Pacific Street, Suite B1
Brooklyn, NY 11217
Email: davidpauleylcsw@gmail.com

David Pauley  

Leonardo da Vinci, Freud, homosexuality, psychoanalysis, homophobia, art history, LGBTQ, biography

Previous considerations of Freud's 1910 pathography of Leonardo da Vinci have grappled mainly with errors of fact (among them a mistranslation in the study's signature childhood memory, widely known since the 1950s). Here a more consequential flaw is examined: Freud's fatefully pathogenic framing of Leonardo's homosexuality. While few present-day analysts share that perspective in its entirety, Freud's complex and plausible reconstruction drew wide support in the literature for more than a century and has to date never been subjected to rigorous critique. A close reading of the study, exploring Freud's perspective and that of later psychoanalysts and historians, seeks to account for the biography's tenacious grip on the psychoanalytic imagination. In the end, it is argued, the pathography is a failed effort to grapple with an unsettling transformation unfolding around and within Freud: the emergence of the category that eventually would be called the “healthy homosexual.”

Cinematic Reflections on the Legacy of the Holocaust: Psychoanalytic Perspectives. Edited by Diana Diamond and Bruce Sklarew. New York: Routledge, 2019, xvi + 253 pp., $155.00 hardcover, $44.95 paperback.

Nancy Goodman

Nancy Goodman

6917 Arlington Road, Suite 220
Bethesda, MD 20814
Email: nrgoodmanphd@gmail.com

Nancy Goodman   Training and Supervising Analyst, Contemporary Freudian Society, Washington, DC.

An interest in the human experience of severe trauma and the Holocaust will bring readers to Cinematic Reflections on the Legacy of The Holocaust: Psychoanalytic Perspectives. The writings about film collected here show the importance of attaining some space in our minds in order to name and process the horrors of genocide and individual suffering. The medium of film provides enough distance from traumatic events, even if that space is minuscule, to allow us at least to approach some knowledge of the horrid unknowable. The written commentaries in this book help us further organize the disorder of annihilation. This double remove from events—first the films and then these essays—lets us breathe when we feel asphyxiated and unable to survive what is being revealed.

The book's editors, Diana Diamond and Bruce Sklarew, have gathered together psychoanalysts who know film and are fearless in their ability to witness trauma. We learn about the “too much” of the Shoah and about the dimensions of thinking itself. Psychoanalytic concepts bring reflection to the representations of the unbearable horrors and atrocities of the Holocaust. After an initial read-through of the chapters, I realized that the writings in this volume bring wisdom to our psychoanalytic work with the psychic helplessness found in traumatized patients' minds and in our own as witnesses. Each chapter provides helpful psychoanalytic understanding of the severe psychic wounds left in our minds after confronting the Holocaust. The writings here inspire belief in psychoanalysis as a treatment to help individuals and society face deeply painful, often unremitting traumas.

Reinstating a free-associative process. Analysts know that the sequelae of trauma cause a shutdown of mind in which symbolization is attacked. The usual movement in the mind from one idea to another, our free-associative process, disappears in the affect storms of traumatization.

Mentalization-Based Treatment for Children: A Time-Limited Approach. By Nick Midgley, Karin Ensink, Karin Lindqvist, Norka Malberg, and Nicole Muller. Washington, DC: American Psychological Association, 2017, xiv + 268 pp., $69.95 hardcover.

Charles Parks

Charles Parks

4701 Willard Avenue, Suite 1223
Chevy Chase, MD 20817
Email: ceparks@me.com

Charles Parks   Associate Clinical Professor of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences, The George Washington University School of Medicine and Health Sciences; Training and Supervising Analyst and Child Supervisor, Washington Baltimore Center for Psychoanalysis; Fellow, American Board of Psychoanalysis.

Mentalization-Based Treatment for Children, by Nick Midgley and colleagues, is divided into two sections. Part 1 focuses on the theoretical

529

framework underlying the mentalization-based approach to treatment (MBT-C), while Part 2 provides a detailed account of the principles and conduct of the proposed treatment.

In Part 1, the authors define mentalization as the “uniquely human ability to interpret the meaning of others' behavior by considering their underlying mental states and intentions, as well as the capacity to understand the impact of one's own affects and behaviors on others” (p. 15). Drawing heavily on the work of Peter Fonagy and his colleagues, the authors describe mentalization as essential to the development of a sense of self and the capacity for self-regulation necessary for successful, emotionally nuanced engagement with others. Carefully linking their conceptualizations to findings from child developmental research, the authors provide a schema of the development of the capacity to mentalize, as well as the parents' role in promoting this capacity, through various stages of child development. The development of childhood psychopathology is linked to a mutually reinforcing series of interactive patterns between parent and child. In these patterns, or cycles, powerful emotions and poor mentalizing lead one party to a relationship to engage in frightening, undermining, frustrating, and distressing and/or coercive interactions with the other party. The behavior of the first party stimulates powerful emotions in the second, engendering further cycles of misunderstanding. Drawing on various areas of study, including the development and treatment of borderline personality disorder in adults, attachment and its disorders, the developmental difficulties of children traumatized by abuse and neglect, and the development of childhood psychopathology, the authors convincingly buttress their argument regarding the substantial contribution to child psychopathology played by failures of mentalization in both parent and child.

Mary Brady's sensitive, thought-provoking books make a pair. The first, The Body in Adolescence, explores the ways that the adolescent's developmental tasks are sometimes met by a turn to the body in symptoms that reflect the experience of psychic isolation, an isolation that is in part a necessary aspect of that development but also entails a delicate tightrope walk across what can feel like a great abyss between the life of the child and emerging adulthood. The second, Analytic Engagements with Adolescents, highlights the adolescent's journey toward discovering adult sexuality and gender experience. Over several chapters in this second book, Brady asks us to contemplate the adolescent's path in this arena, particularly when that path has gone awry. She uses the concept of subversion to address the adolescent's necessary rebellion, as internal and external self- and object relations are reconfigured in an adult body with its new capacities and maturing mind. It is clear that Brady believes in nurturing reflectiveness so that the adolescent becomes increasingly able to use a thoughtful mind in encountering challenges, as opposed to retreating to bodily symptoms such as cutting, drug and alcohol abuse, disordered eating, and suicidality.

535

[[RunningHead]]

The Analytic Couch

On the Couch: A Repressed History of the Analytic Couch from Plato to Freud. By Nathan Kravis. Cambridge: MIT Press, 2017, xx + 204 pp., $29.95 hardcover.

Nancy Crown

Nancy Crown

350 Central Park West, Apt. 2H
New York, NY 10025
Email: ncrownphd@macroweis.com

Nancy Crown   Faculty, Child and Adolescent Psychotherapy Program, William Alanson White Institute; Assistant Clinical Professor, Psychiatry Residency Program, Albert Einstein College of Medicine.

Nathan Kravis's On the Couch: A Repressed History of the Analytic Couch from Plato to Freud was not what I was expecting—and in a fascinating way. In its pages you will not find a history, repressed or otherwise, of the rationale for use of the couch in psychoanalytic practice, nor will you find a summary of arguments made over time for or against its use. Instead, Kravis draws together several interpenetrating influences that, he posits, heavily predisposed Freud, likely at an unconscious level, to adopt the couch as a standard instrument of analytic practice. These influences include art, fashion, philosophy, furniture design, and medicine, along with prevailing cultural trends in Freud's time and before. Kravis uses this multidisciplinary kaleidoscope to explain how the couch became a universal symbol for interiority in general and, in particular, an iconic shorthand for the practice of psychoanalysis. Generously sprinkled throughout the book's pages are beautiful photographs and humorous cartoons featuring the couch in its many incarnations and with its various connotations.

One such connotation, of course, is the association between the couch and the pursuit of self-knowledge. The familiar reasons offered for its use—that it is a holdover of the hypnotic method, or that it stems from Freud's discomfort at having patients stare at him all day—have always been unsatisfying. Some argue (e.g., Starr and Aron 2011) that the emphasis on the couch's central place in hypnosis as the basis for its use in psychoanalysis obscures Freud's association with the little explored practices of electrotherapy and genital stimulation practiced by his colleagues as treatments for hysteria.

541

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Encompassing the Multitude, Animating the Contradictions, and Building Bridges: Reply to Commentaries

Michael Diamond

Michael Diamond

Email: michael@drmichaeljdiamond.com
615 Hanley Way
Los Angeles, CA 90049-1931

Michael Diamond  

Do I contradict myself?

Very well then.… I contradict myself;

I am large. I contain multitudes.

—Walt Whitman (1855, p. 85)

It is quite unexpected to be writing my reply amid these unprecedented times of global pandemic, amid political, healthcare, economic, and environmental instability; widespread protests against systemic racism; and a dismal failure of moral and unifying leadership. This is a harrowing time for many, yet not necessarily traumatic for everyone, for reasons my article addresses in positing trauma's triadic nature (as grounded in economic, intrapsychic, and object-relational understandings).

I will return to trauma and psychoanalysis shortly; however, after reading the commentaries on my article, I am perplexed by my interloutors' ostensible difficulty in understanding certain of my ideas, concepts, and terms. More troubling, though perhaps not surprising given our profession's long-standing schisms, is that two of them either misunderstood or neglected the essence of my thesis, and instead of raising questions that might draw out ideas and clarify any confusion, a straw man argument was created merely to be refuted.

So, as we might anticipate, the concerns addressed in my paper's historical section are repeated in a veritable parallel process that calls to mind Yogi Berra's “It's déjà vu all over again.” In short, as I will elaborate, the commentaries run the risk of deepening schisms where there might otherwise simply be animated tensions arising from the differences that conceptualizing analysts bring to the discussion.

921
